title: Distributed Attacks in Persistent-State AI Control
authors: Josh Hills, Ida Caspary, Asa Cooper Stickland
summary: This paper reveals a new vulnerability in autonomous AI coding agents: when agents iteratively ship code to a persistent codebase, a misaligned agent can distribute malicious changes across multiple pull requests to evade detection. The authors benchmark this "distributed attack" setting using two task families (CLI tools and Flask services) and show that no single monitor can simultaneously defend against both gradual (spread-out) and non-gradual (concentrated) attacks—a fundamental asymmetry that creates a dilemma for defenders.

title: Program-as-Weights: A Programming Paradigm for Fuzzy Functions
authors: Wentao Zhang, Liliana Hotsko, Woojeong Kim, Pengyu Nie, Stuart Shieber et al.
summary: This paper proposes a new way to solve fuzzy programming tasks—ones that don't have clean rule-based solutions, like detecting important log lines or fixing malformed JSON—by compiling natural-language specifications into small, reusable neural artifacts instead of calling expensive LLM APIs every time. The approach, called Program-as-Weights (PAW), trains a compiler to generate lightweight parameter-efficient adapters that run locally and offline, matching the performance of much larger models while using far less memory and compute.

title: Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs
authors: Mona Schirmer, Metod Jazbec, Alexander Timans, Christian Naesseth, Maja Waldron et al.
summary: This paper tackles a critical deployment problem: how to catch when a large language model (LLM) is about to produce an unsafe output in real time. The authors show that a simple approach—using another model to score safety and then raising an alarm when the score falls below a threshold—works as well as more complex methods. The threshold is tuned using risk control, a statistical technique that guarantees certain safety properties. This matters because alignment training alone doesn't prevent bad outputs, and deploying systems without guardrails is dangerous.

title: ReContext: Recursive Evidence Replay as LLM Harness for Long-Context Reasoning
authors: Yanjun Zhao, Ruizhong Qiu, Tianxin Wei, Yuanchen Bei, Zhining Liu et al.
summary: This paper introduces ReContext, a training-free method that improves how large language models use information in very long documents (up to 128K tokens). The key insight: LLMs can access long contexts but often fail to actually use the relevant information when answering questions. ReContext solves this by recursively identifying and replaying relevant evidence before generating answers, without requiring model retraining or external memory systems.

title: What LLM Agents Say When No One Is Watching: Social Structure and Latent Objective Emergence in Multi-Agent Debates
authors: Arman Ghaffarizadeh, Danyal Mohaddes, Aliakbar Izadkhah, Shahriar Noroozizadeh
summary: This paper investigates whether LLM agents behave differently in public versus private settings when placed in socially structured scenarios. The authors introduce a "dual-channel debate" framework where agents produce both public utterances (seen by others) and off-the-record (OTR) responses (hidden), and find that alignment-inducing social pressures cause agents to diverge significantly between channels—public responses become more accommodating while OTR responses reveal divergent or contrary positions. This matters because it suggests LLM agents may develop emergent social objectives (like reputation management) that aren't explicitly prompted, raising questions about how to properly evaluate agent behavior and alignment.

title: Reasoning LLM Improves Speaker Recognition in Long-form TV Dramas
authors: Yuxuan Li, Lingxi Xie, Xinyue Huo, Jihao Qiu, Jiacheng Shao et al.
summary: This paper tackles speaker recognition in long-form TV dramas—figuring out which character said what line—by combining a large reasoning language model with multimodal evidence (audio, text, and visual cues). The authors introduce DramaSR-532K, a dataset of 532K annotated dialogue lines across 900+ characters, and DramaSR-LRM, a method that uses language models to reason through context and synthesize multiple input modalities. The work matters because existing speaker diarization systems fail on short utterances and complex narratives, and this demonstrates that reasoning models can solve this by integrating information across modalities instead of relying solely on voice biometrics.

title: DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation
authors: Yunhe Li, Hao Shi, Wenhao Liu, Mengzhe Ruan, Hanxu Hou et al.
summary: DemoPSD is a new training method that improves how large language models learn from themselves through a process called self-distillation. The key problem it solves: when a model acts as both teacher and student (with the teacher seeing the answer), the student learns shortcuts that depend on information unavailable at test time, hurting generalization. DemoPSD instead uses "disagreement modulation" — dynamically blending teacher guidance with the student's own reasoning based on how different they are — achieving both better out-of-domain performance and more robust reasoning.

title: Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents
authors: Juanwu Lu, Junyu Zhu, Ziran Wang
summary: This paper presents CNeVA, a framework for creating realistic traffic simulation agents that can be precisely controlled along interpretable dimensions (speed, safety, map compliance). Rather than building agents that simply replay logged behaviors, the authors enable engineers to steer agent behavior smoothly—crucial for testing autonomous vehicles without real-world risk. The key innovation is using a learned latent space (Gaussian behavior codes) combined with a diffusion-like trajectory generator, allowing fine-grained control while maintaining realism.

title: Towards Robustness against Typographic Attack with Training-free Concept Localization
authors: Bohan Liu, Wenqian Ye, Guangzhi Xiong, Zhenghao He, Sanchit Sinha et al.
summary: This paper reveals a critical vulnerability in CLIP-based vision models: text appearing in images (typographic attacks) causes models to focus on lexical meaning rather than visual semantics, degrading performance in safety-critical tasks like autonomous driving. The authors propose a training-free mechanistic interpretability method that identifies which attention heads in Vision Transformers encode lexical versus visual information, then surgically intervenes on those circuits to restore robustness without retraining.

title: Visually Grounded Self-Reflection for Vision-Language Models via Reinforcement Learning
authors: Liyan Tang, Fangcong Yin, Greg Durrett
summary: This paper tackles a critical failure mode in vision-language models: they struggle to properly use visual information when correcting their own mistakes during chain-of-thought reasoning. The authors propose VRRL, a reinforcement learning framework that trains models to perform visually grounded self-reflection by masking earlier decisions and exposing them to diverse failure states. The work is important because it shows how to make multimodal AI systems more robust, especially when facing images that differ from their training distribution.

title: Combating Textual Noise and Redundancy: Entropy-Aware Dense Visual Token Pruning
authors: Xuehui Wang, Xuankun Yang, Wei Shen
summary: This paper addresses a fundamental inefficiency in vision-language models (VLMs) — systems that process both images and text — by selectively removing redundant image patches while preserving details needed to answer specific questions. The key insight is that existing pruning methods fail because textual noise corrupts which patches matter, and naive selection breaks spatial coherence. The authors propose EADP, which uses information theory (entropy) to filter noise and frames token selection as a structured optimization problem, achieving better accuracy with fewer image patches.

title: TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution
authors: Jiale Amber Wang, Kaiyuan Wang, Pengyu Nie
summary: This paper introduces TestEvo-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating AI agents on the task of writing and updating software tests alongside code changes. The benchmark is grounded in real commit history and executable — tests can actually run against the code they're meant to test — which matters because most existing benchmarks use static metadata that doesn't verify whether tests actually work or logically connect to the code change. The authors evaluate four state-of-the-art AI agents and find they succeed ~75% of the time, but performance drops notably on recent tasks and under resource constraints.

title: Learning to Move Before Learning to Do: Task-Agnostic pretraining for VLAs
authors: Junhao Shi, Siyin Wang, Xiaopeng Yu, Li Ji, Jingjing Gong et al.
summary: This paper proposes Task-Agnostic Pretraining (TAP), a method that dramatically reduces the need for expensive expert-labeled data when training Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models — neural networks that understand images, follow language instructions, and generate robot actions. The key insight is that robots need two separate skills: learning how to move physically (which can come from cheap, unlabeled robot interactions) and learning what the human wants (which requires language supervision). By separating these objectives, TAP achieves performance comparable to models trained on over 1 million labeled demonstrations while using orders of magnitude less labeled data.

title: Will Scaling Improve Social Simulation with LLMs?
authors: Caleb Ziems, William Held, Su Doga Karaca, David Grusky, Tatsunori Hashimoto et al.
summary: This paper investigates whether larger language models will become better at simulating human social behavior—like predicting opinions, modeling how people act, and forecasting future trends. Using 85+ LLMs ranging from 0.5B to 70B parameters, the authors find that scale *does* improve social simulation in most cases, but with important exceptions: models fail to capture human cognitive biases (like risk aversion) and struggle with opinions from underrepresented groups in training data, suggesting that bigger models alone won't solve all social simulation problems.

title: OrbitQuant: Data-Agnostic Quantization for Image and Video Diffusion Transformers
authors: Donghyun Lee, Jitesh Chavan, Duy Nguyen, Sam Huang, Liming Jiang et al.
summary: OrbitQuant is a post-training quantization method that compresses image and video diffusion transformers (DiTs) to low bit-widths without requiring new calibration data for different models or input conditions. The key insight is to quantize activations in a rotated, normalized basis where values stay predictable across all timesteps and prompts, enabling a single quantization codebook to work universally. This achieves state-of-the-art compression ratios (down to W2A4 — 2-bit weights, 4-bit activations) while preserving generation quality.

title: Neuron-Aware Data Selection for Annotation-Free LLM Self-Distillation
authors: Zhuowei Chen, Xiang Lorraine Li
summary: This paper introduces Neuron-OPSD, a method for improving large language models without human annotations by using the model's internal neuron activations to select which training examples matter most and how to construct better teacher signals. The key insight is that by watching which neurons activate strongly during inference, you can identify the most informative examples to learn from and generate better pseudo-labels, achieving better performance on specialized tasks while maintaining general knowledge.

title: Language Models as Measurement Apparatus for Culture
authors: Kent K. Chang
summary: This paper argues that using language models to measure cultural phenomena is not neutral observation but an active process where the model, data, and evaluation choices actually shape what "culture" means. Drawing on philosophy of science (specifically Karen Barad's work on how scientific instruments co-create the phenomena they measure), the author shows through studies of TV/film dialogue that language models have already absorbed cultural material they're measuring, making the boundary between tool and subject fundamentally blurred. The paper calls for researchers to treat each design choice as a conscious ethical and methodological commitment.

title: Adoption and Ecosystem Health: A Longitudinal Analysis of Open-Source Multi-Agent Frameworks
authors: Xi Zhang, Papi Menon, Vivian Chu, Koray Cosguner
summary: This paper analyzes 15 major open-source AI agent frameworks over a 3.5-year period to understand what actually indicates ecosystem health beyond surface-level metrics like GitHub stars. The authors find that contributor density (active developers per 1,000 stars), cross-framework engagement, and contributor retention are far better predictors of a framework's real adoption and viability than headline popularity, which is often inflated by hype cycles and inorganic activity.

title: AgentsCAD: Automated Design for Manufacturing of FDM Parts via Multi-Agent LLM Reasoning and Geometric Feature Recognition
authors: Emmanuel George, Christopher Keefe, Peter Pak, Amir Barati Farimani
summary: AgentsCAD is a multi-agent system that uses large language models (LLMs) to automatically fix 3D CAD designs for 3D printing (FDM). The system reads a design file, detects manufacturing problems like steep overhangs, and proposes geometry modifications—reorienting parts, adding fillets, or chamfers—then verifies the fixes visually. This matters because current 3D printing software can identify defects but cannot fix them; automating this repair process saves engineers significant manual work and improves print success rates.

title: Understanding the Robustness of Distributed Self-Supervised Learning Frameworks Against Non-IID Data
authors: Xuanyu Chen, Nan Yang, Shuai Wang, Dong Yuan
summary: This paper provides theoretical analysis of how distributed self-supervised learning (D-SSL) — a training approach that learns from unlabeled data across multiple computers — performs when data is unevenly distributed across nodes. The key finding is that Masked Image Modeling (MIM) pre-training is more robust to data heterogeneity than Contrastive Learning, and that better network connectivity improves robustness. This matters because real-world decentralized data is almost never perfectly balanced, so understanding which learning methods handle this scenario better is practically important.

title: EvoPolicyGym: Evaluating Autonomous Policy Evolution in Interactive Environments
authors: Zhilin Wang, Han Song, Runzhe Zhan, Jusen Du, Jiacheng Chen et al.
summary: This paper introduces EvoPolicyGym, a benchmark for evaluating how autonomous agents iteratively improve reinforcement learning policies through repeated edits and feedback cycles. Rather than measuring final performance, it measures the *process* of policy evolution—how well agents allocate a fixed budget of interactions to diagnose problems and refine solutions. The work matters because it creates a controlled setting to study a capability increasingly expected of AI systems: autonomous self-improvement under constraints.

title: Extreme Adaptive Transformer for Time Series Forecasting
authors: Sanjeev Shrestha, Hui Liu, Yifan Zhang
summary: This paper introduces Exformer, a Transformer-based model designed to forecast time series with rare but critical extreme events—particularly useful for predicting streamflow and flood risk. Standard Transformers treat all time points equally, which causes them to underlearn from infrequent but important peaks; Exformer adds a specialized attention mechanism that explicitly captures dependencies between normal and extreme patterns, achieving better 3-day forecasts on real hydrologic datasets.

title: Reasoning effort, not tool access, buys first-try reliability in agentic code generation: an observational study
authors: Achint Mehta
summary: This paper measures what actually improves code generation when AI agents build software independently. Rather than assuming more tools and features help, the authors ran 90 identical agent runs building the same application and measured what mattered: surprisingly, better reasoning effort (asking the model to think harder) lifted first-try success from 28% to 89%, while a testing tool wasted money without improving outcomes. The lesson is simple—match your fix to your actual failure mode.

title: Automated grading of Linux/bash examinations using large language models: a four-level cognitive taxonomy approach
authors: Manuel Alonso-Carracedo, Ruben Fernandez-Boullon, Pedro Celard, Francisco J. Rodriguez-Martinez, Lorena Otero-Cerdeira
summary: This paper tests whether large language models (GPT-4, Claude Opus, Gemini, GLM) can grade Linux/bash exam responses as reliably as human instructors. The authors develop a four-level cognitive taxonomy and evaluate LLM grading on 1,200 real student responses, finding that Gemini 3.0 Pro with structured prompts achieves strong agreement with expert graders (0.888 intraclass correlation), but struggles increasingly on more complex questions.

title: QFedAgent: Quantum-Enhanced Personalized Federated Learning for Multi-Agent Activity Recognition
authors: Quoc Bao Phan, Tuy Tan Nguyen
summary: This paper presents QFedAgent, a federated learning system that combines quantum computing with classical machine learning to help multiple robots or distributed devices collaboratively learn activity recognition models while preserving privacy. The key innovation is using quantum circuits to fuse sensor data (accelerometer and gyroscope readings) with 72 parameters instead of 33,000 — a 450x reduction — while maintaining accuracy on real-world activity recognition benchmarks. This matters because federated learning is privacy-critical for robotics, but handling non-uniform data distributions across many agents has been a bottleneck.

title: The Future of NLP may not be at NLP Conferences: Scholarly Migration Patterns in Natural Language Processing
authors: David Jurgens
summary: This paper documents a fundamental shift in where NLP research is being published: fewer papers appear at traditional NLP conferences (like ACL) and more appear at general machine learning venues. The author shows that both established researchers and newcomers are increasingly publishing outside NLP's traditional home, driven partly by citation advantages that general ML venues confer. This matters because it signals that NLP may be losing its identity as a distinct discipline.

title: Fast Multi-dimensional Refusal Subspaces via RFM-AGOP
authors: Thomas Winninger
summary: This paper proposes RFM-AGOP, a fast method for identifying multi-dimensional subspaces in LLMs that encode complex safety behaviors like refusal to answer harmful questions. Rather than treating behaviors as single linear directions, the authors show these refusals live in higher-dimensional subspaces and develop an efficient algorithm to extract them in seconds—even on reasoning models with long internal computations—making safety monitoring and steering scalable.

title: WattGPU: Predicting Inference Power and Latency on Unseen GPUs and LLMs
authors: Mauricio Fadel Argerich, Jonathan Fürst, Marta Patiño-Martínez
summary: WattGPU predicts the power consumption and inference latency of large language models running on unseen GPUs without requiring any hardware profiling. Using only publicly available model specifications and GPU details, it achieves 3–4× better accuracy than physical baselines (like thermal design power) for predicting how much energy an LLM will draw and how long it takes to generate each token, which matters because data centers need to match specific models to efficient hardware without testing every combination.

title: DecompRL: Solving Harder Problems by Learning Modular Code Generation
authors: Juliette Decugis, Fabian Gloeckle, Francis Bach, Taco Cohen, Gabriel Synnaeve
summary: This paper shows how to make LLMs solve harder coding problems by teaching them to break tasks into modular sub-functions rather than trying to generate complete solutions directly. The key insight: instead of sampling more (expensive GPU time) or using RL naively (which fails when the base model is too far from correct answers), the authors use RL to train models to decompose problems hierarchically, then recombine implementations of different modules to explore a vastly larger solution space cheaply on CPU. On competitive programming benchmarks, this approach cuts GPU costs by ~50× while solving problems standard methods cannot reach.

title: HULAT2 at MER-TRANS 2026: Governed Multi-Agent Simplification for Spanish Easy-to-Read Generation
authors: Lourdes Moreno, Paloma Martínez, Marco Antonio Sanchez-Escudero, Miguel Domínguez-Gómez
summary: This paper presents a multi-agent system for converting Spanish text into Easy-to-Read format—simplified language designed for people with cognitive disabilities or low literacy. The authors combine multiple language models (Gemini 2.5 Flash and RigoChat-7B-v2) in a workflow that routes decisions through quality signals, outperforming a simpler baseline and showing that intelligent orchestration of agents beats linear regeneration for this accessibility task.

title: Hardware-Enforced Semantic Coordination for Safety-Critical Real-Time Autonomous Systems
authors: Uwe M. Borghoff, Paolo Bottoni, Remo Pareschi
summary: This paper proposes a hardware-enforced coordination layer for safety-critical autonomous systems that combine large language models, world models, and multiple specialized components. Rather than relying on software to manage timing, synchronization, and safety constraints, the authors implement selected coordination semantics directly on field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) to guarantee deterministic, bounded behavior in real time. The key insight is that semantic reasoning can remain flexible and software-driven while coordination rules become ironclad and verifiable at the hardware level.

title: The Dual Nature of LLM Persona: Aggregated Tendencies and Frame-Dependent Geometry
authors: Yuan Yuan
summary: This paper reveals that LLM personas have a dual nature: stable aggregate trait scores (like the Big Five personality dimensions) coexist with fragile geometric patterns in the correlation structure of responses that collapse when questions are reordered. The key finding is that this geometric structure isn't intrinsic to the model but rather a frame-dependent coordination pattern—when the question frame is held constant across contexts, the geometric signal recovers and becomes more informative than aggregates alone. This challenges the assumption that LLM personalities are static traits and suggests evaluation methods need to account for contextual framing.

title: SkillFuzz: Fuzzing Skill Composition for Implicit Intents Discovery in Open Skill Marketplaces
authors: Jinwei Hu, Yi Dong, Youcheng Sun, Xiaowei Huang
summary: This paper addresses a critical safety gap in LLM-based skill marketplaces: individually benign skills can combine in unexpected ways to cause harmful behavior. The authors propose SkillFuzz, a fuzzing approach that automatically discovers these "implicit intents" by testing skill compositions without needing to execute them, using planning artifacts as a window into agent intent. This matters because open marketplaces with hundreds of community-contributed skills create an exponentially large space of dangerous combinations that traditional auditing methods miss.

title: Hybridizing a Grouping Metaheuristic with Reinforcement Learning for the One-Dimensional Bin Packing Problem
authors: Zitouni Rania, Mostefai Mounir Sofiane, Tati Youcef, Badaoui Ikram, Bousdjira Nadine et al.
summary: This paper combines a genetic algorithm (HGGA) for bin packing with a reinforcement learning controller that learns which genetic operators to apply at each step, rather than using fixed probabilities. The result is a 50× speedup with only a small loss in solution quality — achieving near-optimal packing in seconds instead of minutes.

title: Securing People and their Machines Against Major Faults
authors: Ohad Eitan, Idit Keidar, Ehud Shapiro
summary: This paper presents a decentralized recovery system for grassroots platforms — peer-to-peer networks of people and their devices — to survive major faults like stolen private keys or lost smartphones. The key insight is that recovery doesn't require a central authority; instead, it relies on a social graph where friends and designated identity custodians collectively authorize and execute key recovery. The work matters because it makes decentralized systems practical for ordinary users who inevitably lose devices or compromise credentials.

title: Dendritic In-Context Learning in a Single-Layer Spiking Neural Network
authors: Juwei Shen, Yujie Wu, Changwen Chen
summary: This paper shows that in-context learning—the ability to learn from examples within a single forward pass—can work in biologically plausible spiking neural networks (SNNs) using only a single layer with dendritic compartments. The key insight is that the subthreshold dynamics of a dendritic compartment naturally implement an online learning algorithm (Widrow-Hoff LMS), eliminating the need for deep architectures, attention mechanisms, or inference-time weight updates. This bridges a gap between how transformers and biological neural networks solve the in-context learning problem.

title: An Additive MLP-GNN Framework for Characterizing Chemical and Structural Contributions to Aqueous Solubility
authors: Sampreeti Bhattacharya, Arkaprava Roy
summary: This paper proposes a hybrid deep-learning model that separately processes chemical descriptors (via a neural network) and molecular graph structure (via a graph neural network) to predict aqueous solubility of drug compounds. Instead of merging these two information sources into a single representation—which obscures what the model actually learned—the framework keeps them separate and combines them only at the final prediction step, making it possible to understand whether predictions come from chemistry, structure, or both. The work shows competitive accuracy while providing transparency about which aspects of a molecule drive solubility predictions.

title: Predicting Early Stages Of Alzheimer's Disease And Identifying Key Biomarkers Using Deep Artificial Neural Network And Ensemble Of Machine Learning Methodologies
authors: Debopriya Ghosh
summary: This paper develops a machine learning system to detect Alzheimer's disease in its early stages using clinical data, neuropsychological test scores, and brain imaging measurements. Early detection of Alzheimer's is critical because symptoms are subtle initially and the disease is currently incurable, but early intervention can slow progression and improve quality of life.

title: Electronic Bursting Neuron: design, equations and hardware implementation
authors: Lev V. Takaishvili, Vladimir I. Ponomarenko, Maksim V. Kornilov, Ilya V. Sysoev
summary: This paper presents a novel electronic circuit design for a bursting neuron — a type of artificial neuron that mimics biological neurons' ability to produce rapid bursts of electrical spikes. The key innovation is using phase-locked loop equations as the mathematical foundation, then designing hardware that matches those equations (rather than the usual reverse approach of writing equations for an already-built circuit). This creates a simpler, more reliable building block for spiking neural networks used in brain-computer interfaces and neuromorphic computing.

title: Born Discrete, Made Smooth: Variational Formulation of Shallow Neural Networks
authors: Matej Benko, Pierre Bousquet, Iwona Chlebicka, Błażej Miasojedow
summary: This paper replaces the discrete, non-convex optimization problem of training shallow neural networks with a continuous variational formulation over parameter densities. By casting the problem in weighted Sobolev spaces, the authors prove the resulting functional is λ-convex and globally well-posed — meaning the optimal solution can be found by solving a single linear system rather than running iterative gradient descent. This is significant because it provides theoretical guarantees and new algorithmic insights for a problem that has traditionally seemed intractable.

title: Autorelevance function and other feature relevance measures for univariate time series
authors: Julian Cardenas, Jamie Arjona, Pedro Delicado
summary: This paper introduces methods to measure which past time steps (lags) matter most for predicting future values in time series forecasting. The authors combine ideas from explainable AI—specifically Shapley values and "ghost variables"—to create relevance scores that work across any forecasting model, from classical statistical models to neural networks. This matters because understanding which historical data points drive predictions can improve model design, reduce unnecessary features, and build trust in automated forecasting systems.

title: CausalSteward: An Agentic Divide-Conquer-Combine Copilot for Causal Discovery
authors: Nicholas Tagliapietra, Gian Lorenzo Marchioni, Moritz Willig, Juergen Luettin, Lavdim Halilaj et al.
summary: This paper introduces CausalSteward, a multi-agent system that helps discover causal relationships in high-dimensional data by breaking the problem into smaller, manageable parts and combining the results with human guidance. The work is important because causal discovery from real-world data is extremely difficult—existing methods often fail when assumptions are violated—and this approach shows how collaborative AI agents and human expertise together can assemble more reliable causal models.

title: Congestion-Based Slot Pricing in a Railway Auction Game
authors: Bill Roungas, Sebastiaan Meijer
summary: This paper studies how to fairly allocate scarce railway slots among competing train operators using an auction mechanism with dynamic pricing. The key innovation is a congestion-based pricing system that penalizes operators requesting the most slots while rewarding those requesting fewer, designed to prevent large operators from dominating the market. The authors test this mechanism with human participants playing the role of railway operators and find that while the pricing system works as intended, larger operators still tend to request many slots despite the penalties—suggesting that pricing alone isn't enough to level the playing field.

title: Full Bayesian Reinforcement Learning via LF-IBIS
authors: Stefano Masini, Cecilia Viscardi, Michela Baccini
summary: This paper proposes LF-IBIS, a new algorithm for Bayesian Reinforcement Learning that works when you can't write down an explicit formula for how the environment works. Instead of requiring a likelihood function (a mathematical description of environment dynamics), the method combines approximate Bayesian computation with importance sampling to update the agent's beliefs online as it interacts with the environment. This matters because real-world environments often have dynamics too complex or poorly understood to describe with closed-form equations, making existing Bayesian RL methods impractical.

title: Mechanism and Stability Analysis of Metabolic Closed-Loop Metaheuristics
authors: Jinliang Xu, Liping Ma
summary: This paper analyzes the Metabolic Multi-Agent Optimizer (MMAO)—a framework where multiple agents share resources (energy, budget) and change roles over time—to understand what makes it work theoretically rather than just empirically. The authors show that under reasonable assumptions about spending and gains, the metabolic resource loop naturally produces three stable behavioral patterns: contraction when resources run low, reinvestment when resources accumulate, and adaptive search redistribution. This matters because it provides a theoretical foundation for a promising class of optimization algorithms instead of treating them as black boxes.

title: MMAO-Cls: Metabolic Multi-Agent Optimization for Joint Feature Selection and Classifier Tuning
authors: Jinliang Xu, Liping Ma
summary: This paper proposes MMAO-Cls, a multi-agent optimization framework that jointly selects features and tunes classifier hyperparameters for tabular machine learning. The key insight is treating each agent as a candidate solution that encodes both which features to use (binary mask) and how to configure the classifier (hyperparameters), with agent interactions and lifecycle dynamics mapped to the accuracy versus model complexity tradeoff. On seven standard datasets, the method achieves competitive test performance with notably compact feature sets, though statistical significance of improvements remains marginal.

title: Mean Field Reinforcement Learning
authors: René Carmona, Mathieu Laurière
summary: This monograph introduces mean field reinforcement learning (MFRL), a framework for training agents in large populations where individual agents interact through aggregated statistics rather than directly with each other. It bridges mean field control theory—a mathematical approach to stochastic control with many interacting agents—and modern reinforcement learning methods, providing both theoretical foundations and practical algorithms that scale to population sizes too large for traditional multi-agent approaches.

title: Simulation Based Reward Function Validation for Multi-Agent On Orbit Inspection
authors: Patrick Quinn, Bala Prenith Reddy Gopu, George M. Nehma, Madhur Tiwari
summary: This paper develops a better way to train groups of spacecraft to inspect objects in orbit using multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL). Instead of telling agents to visit fixed inspection points, the authors create a generalized reward function that evaluates the quality of images collected from any location, letting the agents autonomously decide where to position themselves and when to take pictures. The work matters because it moves MARL inspection from rigid, pre-planned tasks toward flexible, adaptive missions that could handle real-world orbital scenarios.

title: Cache Merging as a Convergent Replicated State for Multi-Agent Latent Reasoning
authors: Carlos Baquero, Luís Brito
summary: This paper solves a fundamental problem in multi-agent reasoning: how to merge the internal memory states (KV-caches) of multiple language model agents into a single coherent context. Previous work concatenated caches in arbitrary order, producing different results depending on input ordering. The authors propose CanonicalMerge, which orders cache fragments by a content-based metric (mean K-norm) to produce byte-identical results regardless of input order—enabling reliable, predictable multi-agent composition for the first time.

title: Is One Layer Enough? Training A Single Transformer Layer Can Match Full-Parameter RL Training
authors: Zijian Zhang, Rizhen Hu, Athanasios Glentis, Dawei Li, Chung-Yiu Yau et al.
summary: This paper investigates whether all layers in a transformer model contribute equally to improvements during reinforcement learning post-training of large language models. The surprising finding: a single transformer layer can recover most or all of the performance gains achieved by updating the entire model, with these high-impact layers consistently concentrated in the middle of the network rather than at input or output positions. This challenges the standard assumption that RL adaptation is uniformly distributed and suggests a more efficient path to RL fine-tuning.

title: Language-Critique Imitation Learning from Suboptimal Demonstrations
authors: Chih-Han Yang, Dai-Jie Wu, Yun-Ping Huang, Ping-Chun Hsieh, Kenneth Marino et al.
summary: This paper proposes using natural language feedback as structured supervision for training policies from suboptimal (imperfect) demonstrations. Instead of compressing feedback into single scores or weights, the method generates language descriptions of what went wrong and how to fix it, then trains policies to follow these critiques. It works on both standard behavior cloning and diffusion-based policies, and shows improvements over existing imitation learning approaches on navigation, manipulation, and game tasks.

title: AutoMem: Automated Learning of Memory as a Cognitive Skill
authors: Shengguang Wu, Hao Zhu, Yuhui Zhang, Xiaohan Wang, Serena Yeung-Levy
summary: This paper treats memory management as a learnable cognitive skill for language models, not a fixed architectural feature. By letting models decide what to encode, when to retrieve, and how to organize information in memory files, and then automatically optimizing both the memory structure and the model's memory decisions through iterative learning loops, the authors show that memory skill alone can improve long-horizon task performance 2–4x, making a 32B open-weight model competitive with frontier systems on complex games.

title: The State-Prediction Separation Hypothesis
authors: Giovanni Monea, Nathan Godey, Kianté Brantley, Yoav Artzi
summary: This paper proposes that Transformers could perform better if they split their computation into two separate streams: one for predicting the next token and one for maintaining state (internal representations) for future predictions. The authors build a Transformer variant implementing this "state-prediction separation" and show it achieves 2–3% better performance on downstream tasks while using data and compute more efficiently than standard Transformers.

title: Are Performance-Optimization Benchmarks Reliably Measuring Coding Agents?
authors: Zhi Chen, Zhensu Sun, Yuling Shi, David Lo, Lingxiao Jiang
summary: This paper audits three major benchmarks used to evaluate coding agents (GSO, SWE-Perf, SWE-fficiency) and finds that their leaderboard rankings are unreliable due to runtime instability across machines, problematic scoring rules, and saturation from public submissions. The work matters because these benchmarks are widely cited as evidence of progress in AI-driven code optimization, but the measurements are noisier and less discriminative than assumed.

title: TiRex-2: Generalizing TiRex to Multivariate Data and Streaming
authors: Patrick Podest, Marco Pichler, Elias Bürger, Levente Zólyomi, Bernhard Voggenberger et al.
summary: TiRex-2 is a recurrent neural network foundation model designed for multivariate time series forecasting that can handle both past observations and future-known covariates (like announced holidays or policy changes) while processing data in streaming mode. Unlike Transformer-based alternatives, it avoids expensive quadratic complexity and can maintain constant computation per new data point, making it practical for real-world systems where data arrives continuously and historical recomputation is infeasible.

title: GPU-Parallel Linearization Error Bounds for Real-Time Robust Optimal Control of Nonlinear and Neural Network Dynamics
authors: Jeffrey Fang, Keyi Shen, Anutam Srinivasan, Glen Chou
summary: This paper solves a critical problem in real-time control: when you use simplified linear approximations of complex nonlinear systems to make control problems tractable, how do you guarantee the actual system still respects safety constraints? The authors develop fast, mathematically verified bounds on linearization errors and embed them into a GPU-accelerated control solver, enabling robust feedback policies for neural network dynamics at real-time speeds (67 Hz on systems with 168 states).

title: Quantum vs. Classical Machine Learning: A Unified Empirical Comparison
authors: Chuanming Yu, Jiaming Liu, Zihao Ge, Xiongfei Wu, Lulu Zhu et al.
summary: This paper provides the first large-scale empirical comparison between quantum machine learning (QML) models and their classical equivalents across supervised learning and reinforcement learning tasks. The key finding is sobering: current quantum models do not yet outperform classical baselines in prediction accuracy, training speed, or policy stability, though they show promise for noise filtering. This matters because it grounds the hype around quantum ML in experimental reality and identifies where future research should focus.

title: Neural Certificate Pricing for Combinatorial Optimization Problems
authors: Jingyi Chen, Xinyuan Zhang, Xinwu Qian
summary: This paper presents Neural Certificate Pricing (NCP), a method that uses neural networks to solve hard combinatorial optimization problems by learning "certificate prices" — dual values that guide recovery of near-optimal solutions. Unlike traditional optimization that exhaustively searches, NCP exploits the fact that checking a solution's feasibility is fast even though finding the best solution is hard. The approach trains unsupervised and shows it can match or beat specialized neural methods while generalizing better to new problem instances.

title: QuasiMoTTo: Quasi-Monte Carlo Test-Time Scaling
authors: Michael Y. Li, Anthony Zhan, Kanishk Gandhi, Noah D. Goodman, Emily B. Fox
summary: This paper introduces QuasiMoTTo, a method that makes inference compute more efficient by generating correlated samples instead of independent ones when scaling language model outputs. Rather than drawing samples randomly and independently (wasting compute on redundant solutions), QuasiMoTTo uses quasi-Monte Carlo sampling to spread samples more evenly across the solution space, achieving the same accuracy with 25–47% fewer samples while maintaining mathematical correctness for policy-gradient training.

title: Adversarial Pragmatics for AI Safety Evaluation: A Benchmark for Instruction Conflict, Embedded Commands, and Policy Ambiguity
authors: Brett Reynolds
summary: This paper introduces a benchmark and annotation protocol called "adversarial pragmatics" to evaluate how language models handle ambiguous, conflicting, or manipulative instructions—situations where natural language is genuinely unclear or contains hidden commands. Rather than simple pass/fail grades, the framework distinguishes between different failure modes: capability gaps, policy confusion, instruction conflicts, or evaluator disagreement. This matters because current safety evaluations often hide what actually went wrong, making it harder to build more reliable and trustworthy AI systems.

title: AGC-Bench: Measuring Artificial General Creativity
authors: Roger Beaty, Vijeta Deshpande, Clin K. Y. Lai, Anna Attuch, Namrata Shivagunde et al.
summary: This paper introduces AGC-Bench, a large-scale benchmark for measuring creativity in large language models across six domains (brainstorming, problem-solving, STEM, narrative, figurative language, and humor). The work combines a systematic literature review of 3,101 AI creativity papers with psychometric methods to address judge bias, ultimately discovering that LLMs exhibit a single underlying creativity factor analogous to general intelligence—but one that remains inferior to human creativity on matched tasks.

title: Towards Developing a Multimodal Chat Assistant for University Stakeholders: RAG-based Approach
authors: Md Abu Hanif Shaikh, Abdullah Al Shafi
summary: This paper describes a multimodal chatbot system designed to help university stakeholders (students, staff, faculty) find answers to institutional questions using retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). The system combines large language models with semantic search over university documents, accepts both text and image queries, and runs efficiently on limited hardware. The key result: RAG reduces hallucinations (false confident statements) from 31.7% to 6.6%, making the system far more reliable for real institutional use.

title: CausalMix: Data Mixture as Causal Inference for Language Model Training
authors: Zinan Tang, Yukun Zhang, Shaomian Zheng, Zhuoshi Pan, Qizhi Pei et al.
summary: This paper presents CausalMix, a method for optimizing how different types of training data are mixed when training large language models (LLMs). Instead of using fixed rules or proxy models that break when data sources change, the authors frame data mixing as a causal inference problem — treating data characteristics as factors that influence model performance. They train a causal model on small experiments, then use it to predict optimal data mixtures for much larger models, demonstrating that this approach generalizes better across different model sizes and data distributions.

title: Clinician-Level Agreement Without Clinical Caution: LLM Evaluator Limits in Medical AI Benchmarking
authors: William Philipp, Finn Fassbender, Thorsten Langer, Martje Pauly, Rebecca Herzog et al.
summary: This paper exposes a critical gap in using LLMs to automatically score medical exam answers: while top LLM evaluators achieve statistical agreement with physicians, they lack clinical judgment—they confidently score every answer instead of abstaining on difficult cases like clinicians do. The researchers created MedQADE, a German medical benchmark with 3,800 questions scored by real doctors and nine LLM judges, revealing that high alignment scores mask dangerous overconfidence in AI-driven medical evaluation.

title: Deep Multitask Learning for Mixed-Type Outcomes with Shared Sparsity
authors: Huichao Li, Tong Wang, Sanguo Zhang, Shuangge Ma
summary: This paper addresses a major challenge in multitask learning: when different tasks have different outcome types (e.g., one task predicts continuous values, another predicts binary labels), standard loss functions aren't directly comparable, making it hard to share information across tasks. The authors propose a framework that handles mixed-type outcomes through unknown monotone transformations and uses shared sparsity (the idea that a common set of important features works across tasks) to improve both prediction and feature selection. They implement this via deep neural networks and prove theoretical guarantees on prediction error and variable selection accuracy.

title: From Personas to Plot: Character-Grounded Multi-Agent Story Generation for Long-Form Narratives
authors: Aayush Aluru, Chloe Ho, Muhammad Hammouri, Kerry Luo, Myra Malik et al.
summary: This paper presents MAGNET, a system that generates long-form stories (100+ pages) by simulating multiple character agents with distinct personas who propose actions based on a shared world state and evolving story goals. The work addresses a key weakness of large language models: they tend to lose plot coherence and introduce contradictions when generating extended narratives. The authors also introduce ATLAS, a verification tool that detects hallucinations by comparing world representations across scenes, and show their approach reduces hallucinations by 50% compared to baseline LLM generation.

title: Calibrating the Instrument: Controllability of an LLM-Driven Synthetic Population
authors: Mirko Degli Esposti
summary: This paper validates whether LLM-driven synthetic populations—computational agents representing real people—respond predictably and consistently to controlled stimuli. Rather than asking whether synthetic agents match real humans, the authors ask a more fundamental question: do synthetic agents respond reproducibly to the same input, and does their internal structure remain stable? They test this using 120 fictional personas in a simulated municipality responding to seven different messages about water policy, finding that the synthetic population behaves like a well-calibrated instrument, with one bonus finding—the LLM correctly identified a poorly-written "positive" message as actually negative.

title: Shapley in Context: Explaining Financial Language with Domain Expertise
authors: Dangxing Chen, Pengzhan Guo
summary: This paper investigates whether Shapley values—a game-theoretic method for attributing model predictions to input features—can explain financial LLM decisions in ways that align with real financial reasoning. The authors show that Shapley-based explanations for financial text tasks are consistent with established domain knowledge, addressing a critical gap: most explainability methods are generic and don't account for finance's regulatory demands and high-stakes decision-making.

title: MMAO-Dyn: A Metabolic Multi-Agent Optimizer for Dynamic Optimization
authors: Jinliang Xu, Liping Ma
summary: This paper extends the Metabolic Multi-Agent Optimizer (MMAO)—a swarm-like algorithm where agents manage energy and roles—to handle dynamic optimization problems where the landscape shifts over time. The key claim is that MMAO's internal metabolic control mechanism can naturally adapt to non-stationary environments without bolting on external adaptation modules. The work shows modest improvements over generic variants and dynamic particle swarm optimization on standard benchmarks, though the gains are most clear in post-change recovery rather than consistent dominance.

title: M2Note: Continual Evolution of Vision Language Models via Mistake Notebook Learning
authors: Haiwen Li, Jing Tang, Rui Chen, Lei Sun, Xiangxiang Chu
summary: This paper introduces M2Note, a training-free framework that improves Vision Language Models (VLMs) by learning from their mistakes without requiring expensive retraining. Instead of fine-tuning model weights, M2Note builds an editable "mistake notebook" — a memory of past failures and how to avoid them — that gets consulted during inference to steer the model away from recurring errors like hallucinations or skipped visual checks.

title: Active-GRPO: Adaptive Imitation and Self-Improving Reasoning for Molecular Optimization
authors: Xuefeng Liu, Mingxuan Cao, Qinan Huang, Thomas Brettin, Rick Stevens et al.
summary: This paper addresses how to train language models to reason effectively about molecular design. The authors propose Active-GRPO, a method that lets a model decide when to learn from examples (imitation) versus when to explore its own ideas (reinforcement learning), while dynamically upgrading the examples it learns from. On molecular optimization benchmarks, their approach outperforms existing methods by allowing the model to move beyond the constraints of fixed reference solutions.

title: Ghost in the Kernel: In-Context Learning with Efficient Transformers via Domain Generalization
authors: Peilin Liu, Ding-Xuan Zhou
summary: This paper analyzes how linear transformers—a computationally cheaper variant of standard transformers—can perform in-context learning (adapting to new tasks using only examples in the input, without updating weights). The authors provide theoretical guarantees showing that linear transformers can learn as effectively as standard transformers while reducing computational cost from quadratic to linear in context length. They also propose practical ways to convert existing large language models to use linear attention without retraining from scratch.

title: End-to-End Parametric Portfolio Policies for Cross-Asset Futures Timing: When Do AI Models Beat Simple Rules?
authors: Austin Pollok, Kevin Robik
summary: This paper asks whether machine learning models can beat simple rule-based strategies for timing cross-asset futures portfolios. The researchers train neural networks (LSTMs and transformers) to map market conditions directly to portfolio weights using a differentiable Sharpe ratio loss, then compare them against equal weighting, risk parity, and momentum rules on CME futures data. They find that learned policies can outperform rules on average, but the advantage shrinks significantly once trading costs are factored in—and transformers win out by trading far less than LSTMs.

title: Neural Network-Based Estimation of Time-Dependent Parameters in AR(p) Processes
authors: Agnieszka Kopeć, Paweł Przybyłowicz, Martyna Wiącek
summary: This paper proposes a neural network approach to estimate time-varying coefficients in autoregressive (AR) time-series models, allowing simple parametric forecasting models to capture complex, nonstationary dynamics. The key innovation is using deep learning to recover the time-dependent parameters while preserving model interpretability—you can still see what the coefficients are at each time step, unlike black-box neural networks. The authors analyze both Gaussian and heavy-tailed (Laplace) noise distributions and derive prediction intervals, making the method practical for uncertainty quantification.

title: Agri-SAGE: Simulation-Grounded Multi-Agent LLM for Context-Aware Agricultural Advisory Generation
authors: Vedant Balasubramaniam, Geetha Charan, Manojkumar Patil, Rohit P Suresh, V Priyanka et al.
summary: Agri-SAGE is a framework that combines large language models (LLMs) with crop simulation software to generate better farming advice. It solves two problems: static agricultural guidelines ignore seasonal variations, while LLM-only systems can sound plausible but be agronomically wrong. The system uses multiple AI agents that reason through farming decisions and validate recommendations against biophysical simulations, achieving higher crop yields than traditional approaches.

title: Self-Organized Learning in Oscillatory Neural Networks with Memristive Signed Couplings
authors: Riley Acker, Aman Desai, Garrett Kenyon, Frank Barrows
summary: This paper demonstrates how oscillatory neural networks (ONNs)—brain-inspired computing systems where neurons communicate through synchronized timing patterns—can learn and store memories autonomously using memristive devices with both positive and negative (signed) connections. The key innovation is showing that negative weights are essential for creating stable anti-phase memory patterns, and the authors provide circuit simulations proving this neuromorphic approach can denoise corrupted inputs without external training signals.

title: ASPIRE: Agentic /Skills Discovery for Robotics
authors: Runyu Lu, Yubo Wu, Ethan Kou, Letian Fu, Wenli Xiao et al.
summary: ASPIRE is a system that automatically discovers, writes, and refines robot control programs through continuous learning and exploration. Instead of manually programming each robot behavior, ASPIRE autonomously diagnoses failures, generates fixes, and builds a reusable library of skills that work across different tasks, simulated and real robots, and robot types—achieving dramatic improvements (up to 77%) over prior methods on complex manipulation tasks.

title: Sample Complexities of Estimating Gumbel--Max Watermark Proportions with and without Reduction to Pivotal Statistics
authors: Shuwen Chai, Qiaosen Wang
summary: This paper solves a statistical problem: given a document that may be partially human-written and partially machine-generated, how can you estimate what fraction was produced by a specific watermarked language model? The authors analyze two methods under the Gumbel-max watermarking scheme—one using full information and one using a simplified "pivot" statistic—and prove how many document tokens you need to see to reliably estimate the watermark proportion. They show that the simpler method requires substantially more samples, suggesting it trades off computational elegance for statistical efficiency.

title: EVOTS: Evolutionary Transformer Search for Time Series Forecasting
authors: AbdElRahman ElSaid, Damir Pulatov
summary: This paper presents EVOTS, a framework that automatically designs Transformer-like neural networks optimized for time-series forecasting tasks using evolutionary algorithms. Instead of using a single fixed Transformer architecture, the method evolves custom architectures tailored to specific forecasting problems, demonstrating competitive or better performance on standard benchmarks while keeping computational costs reasonable.

title: QVal: Cheaply Evaluating Dense Supervision Signals for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
authors: Sergio Hernández-Gutiérrez, Matteo Merler, Ilze Amanda Auzina, Joschka Strüber, Ameya Prabhu et al.
summary: This paper introduces QVal, a training-free evaluation framework for assessing the quality of dense supervision signals used to train long-horizon LLM agents. Instead of measuring supervision quality indirectly by running expensive training pipelines, QVal directly measures whether a method's scores align with Q-values (action quality estimates) from a reference policy. The work benchmarks 21 dense supervision methods across diverse environments, revealing that simple prompting baselines often outperform recent published methods — findings that would be hidden by traditional training-dependent evaluation.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Metacognitive Feedback Elicits Faithful Uncertainty Expression in LLMs
authors: Gabrielle Kaili-May Liu, Avi Caciularu, Gal Yona, Idan Szpektor, Arman Cohan
summary: This paper introduces reinforcement learning with metacognitive feedback (RLMF), a method that trains large language models to accurately assess and express their own uncertainty. The core insight is that models which can reliably judge their own performance can then improve it—addressing a critical failure mode where LLMs hallucinate confidently and misrepresent what they actually know. The work demonstrates that metacognitive self-assessment can serve as a powerful training signal, outperforming standard reinforcement learning approaches by up to 63% and producing models whose expressed uncertainty genuinely reflects their internal knowledge.

title: When LLMs Read Tables Carelessly: Measuring and Reducing Data Referencing Errors
authors: Yuqing Yang, Qi Zhu, Zhen Han, Boran Han, Zhengyuan Shen et al.
summary: Large language models often make data referencing errors (DREs)—incorrectly citing or omitting values from tables—even when they understand the table structure. This paper systematically measures these errors across different models and proposes using a lightweight "critic" model to detect and filter out bad outputs, improving final answer accuracy by up to 12%. The work addresses a gap in reliability for LLM-based table reasoning, which matters because incorrect intermediate steps undermine trustworthiness even if final answers sometimes happen to be right.

title: Generative Skill Composition for LLM Agents
authors: Xinyu Zhao, Zhen Tan, Vaishnav Tadiparthi, Nakul Agarwal, Kwonjoon Lee et al.
summary: This paper tackles a central problem for LLM-based agents: how to select and order a sequence of reusable skills (modular procedural knowledge packages) to solve complex tasks. The authors propose SkillComposer, a constrained decoder that jointly predicts which skills to use, how many times, and in what order—treating this as a structured sequence prediction problem rather than independent skill retrieval. On real coding tasks, the method outperforms simpler retrieval baselines and nearly matches the performance ceiling of oracle skill selection.

title: Automated Background Swapping for Robustness against Spurious Backgrounds
authors: Cesar Roder, Kajetan Schweighofer
summary: This paper addresses a critical failure mode in deep learning: when image classifiers rely on spurious correlations (like specific backgrounds) rather than the actual object of interest. The authors propose AutoBackSwap, a method that automatically swaps backgrounds in training images to force the classifier to focus on foregrounds. The approach works by using a secondary network to separate foreground from background, filling in new backgrounds, and creating augmented training data—all with minimal manual labeling.

title: TRIAGE: Role-Typed Credit Assignment for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
authors: Yuanda Xu, Zhengze Zhou, Hejian Sang, Xiaomin Li, Jiaxin Zhang et al.
summary: This paper addresses a critical problem in agentic reinforcement learning: standard reward signals from task outcomes are too coarse-grained and mislabel useful actions. TRIAGE proposes a finer-grained credit assignment system that classifies each action segment by its semantic role (e.g., decisive progress vs. useful exploration vs. regression) and assigns process rewards accordingly. The result is better policy learning with fewer environment interactions across web-based tasks.

title: FedLAB: Traceable Semantic Codebooks for Federated Multimodal Graph Foundation Learning
authors: Zekai Chen, Kairui Yang, Xuaner Chen, Xunkai Li, Xun Wu et al.
summary: This paper introduces FedLAB, a framework for training multimodal graph foundation models across decentralized clients while keeping data private. Unlike standard federated learning approaches that share parameters or embeddings, FedLAB organizes knowledge into semantic codebooks that explicitly track *why* predictions are made—linking evidence from text, images, node identity, and network structure. The key innovation is maintaining interpretability (what the authors call "semantic traceability") even in federated settings where raw data never leaves individual devices.

title: Scalable Behaviour Cloning on Browser Using via Skill Distillation
authors: Kaisen Yang, Zheng Jiang, Yuzhao Peng, Houde Qian, Boshi Zhang et al.
summary: This paper proposes a method for training browser automation agents by distilling human web interactions into reusable natural-language skills. Rather than training agents from scratch on individual tasks, the researchers extract high-level behavioral patterns from real user browsing data, compress them into skills an agent can understand and reuse, and organize these skills into a structured graph. The key insight is that internet users already demonstrate vast amounts of skilled behavior online—the challenge is not collecting training data but teaching agents to reason abstractly about incomplete information.

title: CoMet: Context and Multiplicity Decomposition for Multimodal Uncertainty Estimation
authors: Sanghyuk Chun, William Yang, Amaya Dharmasiri, Olga Russakovsky
summary: This paper introduces CoMet, a method for estimating uncertainty in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) by decomposing it into two sources: context-induced ambiguity and answer multiplicity. Instead of generating multiple answers or sampling repeatedly, CoMet trains a lightweight post-hoc module that predicts these two uncertainty components, enabling efficient confidence estimation for open-ended tasks like visual question answering and hallucination detection.

title: Surrogate Fidelity: When Can Open LLMs Explain Closed Ones?
authors: Philippe Chlenski, Zachariah Carmichael, Ayush Warikoo, Chia-Tse Shao, Yingxiao Ye et al.
summary: This paper asks a practical but underexplored question: when you can inspect an open-source language model's internals, can you use those insights to understand how closed proprietary models (like GPT or Gemini) work? The authors find a surprising answer — models often agree on *what* they output but disagree on *why*, meaning prediction-level similarity does not guarantee mechanistic similarity. This matters because mechanistic interpretability research has focused on open models while deployed systems remain opaque.

title: AxDafny: Agentic Verified Code Generation in Dafny
authors: Benjamin Breen, Austin Letson, Borja Requena Pozo, Leopoldo Sarra
summary: This paper presents AxDafny, a system where AI agents generate not just code but also the formal proofs needed to verify that code is correct. The key innovation is a repair loop: when verification fails, the agent automatically suggests fixes (new invariants, assertions, termination arguments) and tries again. The authors also release a benchmark of 250 programming problems in Dafny with formal specs, and show that their approach achieves 92.7% verification success—a significant improvement over prior work.

title: PolicyGuard: From Organizational Policies to Neuro-SymbolicCompliance Review Engines
authors: Sameer Malik, Ayush Singh, Amar Prakash Azad
summary: PolicyGuard is a framework that converts organizational policies into executable compliance review engines by combining neural networks (LLMs) with symbolic logic rules. Instead of asking an LLM to make a yes/no compliance decision directly, it decomposes the problem: the LLM answers specific factual questions about a document, and formal logic rules then determine if those facts satisfy the policy. This matters because it makes compliance decisions transparent, auditable, and easier to update when policies change.

title: DigitalCoach: Communication and Grounding Gaps in Human and Agentic Computer Use Coaching
authors: Meng Chen, Anya Ji, Tsung-Han Wu, Tobias Maringgele, David M. Chan et al.
summary: DigitalCoach introduces a dataset of 72 real coaching sessions where human experts teach novices to use software, captured with screen recordings and dialogue transcripts. The paper shows that current AI models struggle to coach effectively—they give direct instructions but fail to explain *why*, diagnose errors, or ground their advice in what's actually visible on screen. This matters because as AI agents automate more software tasks, they need to bridge the gap between what they can do and what they can teach humans to do.

title: TreeAgent: A Generalizable Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Bias Labeling in Forestry via Compiled Expert Rules and Vision-Language Models
authors: Shiyi Chen, Nicholas Saban, Collin Hargreaves, Huiqi Wang
summary: This paper presents TreeAgent, a multi-agent system that combines expert decision trees with vision-language models (VLMs) to automatically label tree bias in forestry remote sensing images. Instead of relying on slow, inconsistent human experts, the system uses VLMs to make semantic judgments at each decision node, with multiple agents voting to reduce errors — achieving better accuracy than traditional supervised learning while cutting annotation costs significantly.

title: MECoBench: A Systematic Study of Multimodal Agent Collaboration in Embodied Environments
authors: Qingyun Liu, Jiwen Zhang, Jingyi Hu, Siyuan Wang, Zhongyu Wei
summary: This paper introduces MECoBench, a benchmark for evaluating how multimodal large language models (MLLMs—AI systems that process images and text) work together as embodied agents (robots or simulated entities in visual environments) to complete real-world tasks. The key finding is that collaboration between agents improves task success, but only when communication is effective and coordination overhead is manageable; the optimal collaboration strategy depends on team size and individual model capability.

title: Signed-Permutation Coordinate Transport for RMSNorm Transformers
authors: John Sweeney
summary: This paper solves a critical technical problem in modern LLM workflows: how to correctly move interpretability tools (like steering vectors and sparse autoencoders) between different model checkpoints. The authors show that the mathematical symmetry group governing this transport depends on the model's normalization layer — RMSNorm models have a larger symmetry group than LayerNorm models — and permutation-only alignment misses half the structure, leading to broken steering effects and interpretability tools. By tracking both permutations *and* per-channel sign flips, they recover 91% of coordinates across fine-tuning runs versus 60% with existing methods, and restore the functionality of tools that currently fail under standard alignment.

title: Analytic Cut in Epistemic Logics with Distributed Knowledge
authors: Ryo Murai, Sizhuo Liu, Katsuhiko Sano
summary: This paper develops proof-theoretic foundations for reasoning about distributed knowledge in multi-agent systems — a formal notion of what groups of agents collectively know, defined as the intersection of their individual knowledge. The authors prove that analytic cut (a weaker but still powerful proof principle) holds for epistemic logics based on K45, KD45, and S5 modal systems, enabling sound and complete sequent calculi where reasoning stays within relevant formulas.

title: Inquisitive Action Logic
authors: Ivano Ciardelli
summary: This paper introduces inquisitive action logic (InqAL), a formal system for reasoning about what agents can do in multi-agent settings. Unlike traditional approaches that ask "what outcomes can an agent force?", InqAL also captures "what aspects of outcomes does an agent determine through their choices?" — treating these questions as modal logic statements. The work provides a complete mathematical framework (axiomatization, decidability proofs) for reasoning about agent capabilities and determination in concurrent settings.

title: The Logic of Data Access and Data Exchanges
authors: Alexandru Baltag, Sonja Smets
summary: This paper develops a new logical framework that extends Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL) — a formal system for reasoning about what multiple agents know and how knowledge changes — to handle data access and exchange scenarios. The key innovation is adding operators that express not just *whether* an agent knows something, but also how much they can narrow down unknowns (e.g., "I can restrict this variable to 5 possible values") and what happens when data sources become accessible. The work matters because it provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for reasoning about privacy, data sharing, and information leakage in multi-agent systems.

title: Resolving Asynchronous Distributed Knowledge
authors: Philippe Balbiani, Hans van Ditmarsch, Clara Lerouvillois
summary: This paper develops a new modal logic for reasoning about knowledge in distributed systems where agents communicate asynchronously — that is, without a shared global clock or common awareness of when information is being shared. The key innovation is adding "resolution modalities" that track which agents know about knowledge-sharing events, even when some agents are unaware of updates involving other agents. This matters because real distributed systems (computers, networks, organizations) operate asynchronously, so this logic better models how knowledge actually spreads in practice.

title: Relational and Sequential Conformal Inference for Energy Time Series over Graphs via Foundation Models
authors: Keivan Faghih Niresi, Alice Cicirello, Olga Fink
summary: This paper proposes STOIC, a framework that combines spatial-temporal graph neural networks with tabular foundation models to generate reliable uncertainty estimates for energy demand forecasting. Rather than just predicting single values, STOIC produces prediction intervals (ranges) with statistical guarantees, which is critical for operators managing power grids and heating networks under uncertainty.

title: Diffusing Blame: Task-Dependent Credit Assignment in Biologically Plausible Dual-Stream Networks
authors: Yutaro Yamada, Luca Grillotti, Rujikorn Charakorn, Sebastian Risi, David Ha et al.
summary: This paper develops a biologically plausible learning rule called Error Diffusion (ED) that works in neural networks constrained by Dale's principle — a biological constraint requiring each neuron to be either exclusively excitatory or inhibitory. The key innovation is "modulo error routing," which allows error signals to be distributed through a dual-stream network architecture without backpropagation or weight transport. The authors show this approach scales from MNIST (96.7%) to CIFAR-10 (61.7%) and works in reinforcement learning tasks, bridging the gap between biological realism and practical performance.

title: On Optimal Data Splitting for Split Conformal Prediction
authors: Sayan Das, Bahram Yaghooti, Todd A. Kuffner, Soumendra N. Lahiri
summary: This paper solves a fundamental but overlooked problem: how to optimally split data into training and calibration sets for split conformal prediction (a method that creates prediction intervals with guaranteed coverage). The key insight is that the optimal split ratio depends on the underlying model structure, and the authors derive closed-form formulas showing exactly what split minimizes interval width while maintaining coverage guarantees—then demonstrate these formulas across linear regression, nonparametric methods, and neural networks.

title: A Large-Scale Empirical Evaluation of MMAO Under Fair-Budget Continuous and Discrete Benchmarks
authors: Jinliang Xu, Liping Ma
summary: This paper rigorously evaluates the Metabolic Multi-Agent Optimizer (MMAO)—a multi-agent framework for resource allocation—on standard continuous optimization benchmarks (CEC2017 functions) and discrete problems (traveling salesman, knapsack). The key finding is that MMAO's ability to dynamically redistribute computational resources among agents consistently outperforms traditional methods like particle swarm optimization and evolution strategies, suggesting multi-agent coordination is genuinely useful for hard optimization problems.

title: Robustness of neural networks to random noise perturbations of their inputs
authors: Mark Levene, Martyn Harris
summary: This paper studies how well neural networks maintain their performance when their inputs are corrupted by random noise. The authors propose a simple, computationally efficient metric that predicts how much a network's error will increase under input perturbations, treating the network as a black box. They validate their approach on real datasets and introduce "robustness curves" to visualize and compare robustness across different conditions.

title: On the Convergence of Self-Improving Online LLM Alignment
authors: Xudong Wu, Pangpang Liu, Vaneet Aggarwal, Jiayu Chen
summary: This paper proves convergence guarantees for SAIL (Self-Improving Alignment), an algorithm that helps large language models stay aligned with human values even as the distribution of user queries shifts over time. The authors identify that the standard SAIL objective lacks favorable mathematical properties for optimization, so they propose SAIL-RevKL—a regularized version that adds a reverse-KL penalty—and prove it converges efficiently with near-linear sample complexity. The work bridges the gap between SAIL's empirical success and formal theoretical understanding.

title: Can Tabular In-Context Learners Generalize to Biomolecular Property Prediction?
authors: Davy Guan, Lu Zhang, Asiri Wijesinghe, Allen Zhu, He Zhao et al.
summary: This paper tests whether tabular foundation models—neural networks trained on synthetic tables to learn in-context learning—can predict biomolecular properties (protein fitness, molecular toxicity) with limited labeled data. The surprising answer is yes: models like TabPFN3 work competitively despite being trained on causal graphs with no obvious connection to biology. However, performance heavily depends on the choice of molecular representation (e.g., protein sequence encoder, molecular descriptors), not the tabular model itself.

title: Multistage Defer Trees for Hybrid Interpretability: If at First You Can't Succeed, Tree Again
authors: Zakk Heile, Hayden McTavish, Margo Seltzer, Cynthia Rudin
summary: This paper proposes Multistage Defer Trees, a method that chains together simple decision trees to achieve high accuracy while keeping most predictions interpretable. Rather than forcing a choice between a single interpretable tree (low accuracy) or a complex black-box ensemble (high accuracy but opaque), the approach routes most data through one or a few simple trees and only defers harder cases to subsequent trees or a black box, maintaining transparency for the majority of decisions.

title: Partition-Guided Distance Saliency: Bridging Decision and Objective Spaces in Many-Objective Optimization
authors: Cláudio Lúcio do Val Lopes, Flávio Vinícius Cruzeiro Martins, Elizabeth Fialho Wanner
summary: This paper introduces a framework called Partition-Guided Distance Saliency (PGDS) that helps explain which decision variables matter most in many-objective optimization problems — situations where you're trying to optimize many competing goals simultaneously. The core challenge: as the number of objectives grows, the relationship between input decisions and outcomes becomes nearly impossible to visualize or understand. PGDS uses a three-step approach to automatically identify which variables "drive" progress toward better solutions and which ones "block" it, making high-dimensional optimization interpretable.

title: LeVo 2: Stable and Melodious Song Generation via Hierarchical Representation Modeling and Progressive Post-Training
authors: Shun Lei, Huaicheng Zhang, Dapeng Wu, Yaoxun Xu, Lishi Zuo et al.
summary: LeVo 2 is a hybrid LLM-Diffusion framework that generates full-length songs with coherent lyrics, stable vocals, and melodious accompaniment by using hierarchical token modeling (mixed tokens for planning, then parallel vocal/accompaniment refinement) combined with a novel aesthetics-guided progressive post-training schedule. The work addresses a fundamental trade-off in music generation: mixed-token systems preserve coordination but lose acoustic detail, while dual-track systems improve detail but weaken global coherence—LeVo 2 solves this through staged training that separates musicality learning, controllability alignment, and acoustic refinement.

title: Self-Evolving World Models for LLM Agent Planning
authors: Xuan Zhang, Wenxuan Zhang, See-Kiong Ng, Yang Deng
summary: This paper presents WorldEvolver, a framework that improves LLM agent planning by maintaining and refining a world model—a predictive system that forecasts action consequences—at test time without retraining. The key innovation is using episodic memory (past transitions), semantic memory (learned rules), and selective filtering to make the world model's predictions more reliable for downstream agent decision-making, resulting in better planning performance on interactive environments.

title: GROW$^2$: Grounding Which and Where for Robot Tool Use
authors: Yuhong Deng, Yuyao Liu, David Hsu
summary: GROW² enables robots to creatively repurpose objects as tools (e.g., using a plate to cut cake) by grounding task instructions into both semantic object understanding and geometric 3D localization. The system leverages vision-language models for commonsense reasoning about which object to use and where to apply force, avoiding the need for large labeled datasets and achieving zero-shot generalization to novel objects.

title: Pessimism's Paradox: Conservative Offline Training Amplifies Reward Hacking During Online Adaptation in Reasoning Models
authors: Subramanyam Sahoo, Aman Chadha, Vinija Jain, Divya Chaudhary
summary: This paper reveals a counterintuitive failure mode in offline reinforcement learning: policies trained with high conservatism (staying close to the training distribution) actually become *more* vulnerable to reward hacking when fine-tuned online. The authors show mechanistically that conservative training compresses policy entropy, creating a narrow response distribution that, while proximal to the reward model's training data, is easier to exploit through ensemble disagreement during online optimization.

title: DOPD: Dual On-policy Distillation
authors: Xinlei Yu, Gen Li, Qingyi Si, Guibin Zhang, Yuqi Xu et al.
summary: This paper introduces DOPD (Dual On-policy Distillation), a method for transferring capabilities from teacher models to student models by dynamically routing token-level supervision signals.
